mcqs.question: If 51 students in a class are born in the same year, how many must share the same birth month?
mcqs.explanation: Using generalized pigeonhole principle: ⌈51/12⌉ = ⌈4.25⌉ = 5. So at least 5 students share the same birth month.

mcqs.question: What is the minimum number of cards to be drawn from a deck of 52 to guarantee at least 2 cards of the same suit?
mcqs.explanation: There are 4 suits (pigeonholes). Drawing 5 cards (pigeons) guarantees at least 2 cards in the same suit by pigeonhole principle.

mcqs.question: In a class of 30 students, what is the minimum number of students who share the same first letter of their first name? (Assume English alphabet)
mcqs.explanation: There are 26 letters. With 30 students, ⌈30/26⌉ = 2. So at least 2 students share the same starting letter.

mcqs.question: How many people must be present in a room to guarantee that at least two have birthdays on the same day of the week?
mcqs.explanation: There are 7 days in a week (pigeonholes). To guarantee repetition: ⌈n/7⌉ ≥ 2 means n ≥ 8. So 8 people guarantee same weekday birthday.

mcqs.question: The Pigeonhole Principle is also known as:
mcqs.explanation: The principle is named after German mathematician Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet, also called Dirichlet Drawer Principle or Schubfachprinzip in German.

mcqs.question: What is the minimum number of socks to pick from a drawer containing only black and white socks to guarantee a matching pair in darkness?
mcqs.explanation: With 2 colors (pigeonholes), picking 3 socks guarantees at least 2 of the same color by pigeonhole principle.

mcqs.question: How many integers from 1 to 100 must be selected to guarantee two numbers whose sum is 101?
mcqs.explanation: Pairs that sum to 101: (1,100), (2,99), ..., (50,51) - 50 pairs. Pick 51 numbers and by pigeonhole, one complete pair is selected. Actually need 52 to guarantee the pair is complete from the same pair group.

mcqs.question: What is the maximum number of edges in a graph with 6 vertices that can avoid containing a triangle?
mcqs.explanation: By Mantel's theorem (application of pigeonhole/Ramsey), a triangle-free graph on n vertices has at most ⌊n²/4⌋ edges. For n=6, max edges = ⌊36/4⌋ = 9.

mcqs.question: In a 3x3 grid, if you place 10 tokens, what is guaranteed?

options.text: At least one row has 4 tokens
At least one column has 4 tokens
At least one row or column has at least 4 tokens

mcqs.explanation: There are 6 lines (3 rows + 3 columns). By pigeonhole: ⌈10/6⌉ = 2, but with 10 tokens, ⌈10/3⌉ = 4 for some lines. Actually, since 10 > 3×3, one row or column must have at least 4 tokens.

mcqs.question: How many people are needed to guarantee that there are at least 3 with the same birth month?
mcqs.explanation: Using formula: to guarantee k in one box with m boxes, need (k-1)m + 1. Here k=3, m=12: (3-1)×12 + 1 = 25. Wait, that's minimum to get at least 3 in one month. Actually 26 gives guaranteed 3 by generalized principle.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT an application of Pigeonhole Principle?
mcqs.explanation: Prim's algorithm is a greedy algorithm for finding minimum spanning tree. It doesn't use pigeonhole principle. The others directly apply the principle.

mcqs.question: What is R(3,3), the Ramsey number for two triangles?
mcqs.explanation: R(3,3) = 6 means in any 2-coloring of edges of K₆, there is always a monochromatic triangle. This is a classic pigeonhole principle extension.

mcqs.question: A committee of 10 people has a chair and vice-chair. If 4 are women and 6 are men, how many ways to select such that at least one woman is in the committee?
mcqs.explanation: Total ways to choose 2 people: C(10,2). Ways with only men: C(6,2). Subtract to get at least one woman: C(10,2) - C(6,2).

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity lower bound for comparison-based sorting?
mcqs.explanation: By information-theoretic argument using pigeonhole principle: n! possible permutations need log₂(n!) ≥ n log₂ n - 1.44n comparisons in worst case. Hence Ω(n log n).

mcqs.question: How many people needed to guarantee 3 of same gender in a room?
mcqs.explanation: With 2 genders (pigeonholes), to guarantee 3 in one: (3-1)×2 + 1 = 5. So 5 people guarantee at least 3 of same gender.

mcqs.question: The generalized pigeonhole principle states that if n items are put into m boxes, then at least one box contains at least:
mcqs.explanation: The ceiling function ⌈n/m⌉ gives the minimum maximum. At least one box must contain at least ⌈n/m⌉ items by the generalized pigeonhole principle.

mcqs.question: In a bag with red, blue, and green balls (unlimited), what is minimum picks to guarantee 5 of same color?
mcqs.explanation: Worst case: pick 4 of each color = 12 balls, still no 5. One more pick (13) guarantees 5 of some color. Formula: (k-1)m + 1 = (5-1)×3 + 1 = 13.

mcqs.question: What does the Erdos-Szekeres theorem state about sequences?

options.text: Every sequence of (r-1)(s-1)+1 numbers has increasing of length r or decreasing of length s

mcqs.explanation: Erdos-Szekeres theorem: Any sequence of (r-1)(s-1)+1 distinct numbers has either an increasing subsequence of length r or decreasing subsequence of length s.

mcqs.question: If a hash table has 100 slots and we insert 101 keys, what is guaranteed?

options.text: At least one collision will occur

mcqs.explanation: By pigeonhole principle: 101 items (pigeons) into 100 slots (holes) guarantees at least one slot has 2 items, i.e., a collision.

mcqs.question: What is the minimum number of integers to select from 1-50 to guarantee a pair summing to 51?
mcqs.explanation: Pairs summing to 51: (1,50), (2,49), ..., (25,26) - 25 pairs. Select 26 numbers and by pigeonhole, you might get one complete pair. Actually need 27 to guarantee a complete pair from same pair group.
